entry.title: Project Kernseife - improving the measurement of Clean Core in ABAP
entry.content:
TL;DR

To improve the handling and measuring of classic ABAP code in ATC with regards to clean core, the possibility of an ATC check based on a custom classification was developed and is now published as an open source project named "Project Kernseife".

 

Challenges in Measuring Clean Core

Working in the area of clean core - especially focusing on the dimension "extensibility" - in projects of different scales, we were more than once asked in the last years on how to measure and govern the clean core principles within SAP S/4HANA systems (focusing on Private Cloud & On-Premises).

A key part to describe the "clean core state" of extensions is from our point of view the creation of a technical debt score that should align with the governance process in your system. That is why SAP's own code quality tool ABAP Test Cockpit (ATC) is very helpful on this. Since the release of "ABAP Cloud" as target for On-Stack extensions, we have now possibibilities with the related ATC Check Variant (ABAP_CLOUD_READINESS) to identify the "distance to Tier 1".

But looking at landscapes with large amount of legacy code (e.g. > 100,000 custom code objects in classic ABAP) or systems where you cannot use sufficiently released APIs for Tier 1, it becomes more difficult to identify the "real" technical debt.

If you run with the default ABAP_CLOUD_READINESS variant into larger systems, you might be overwhelmed by a huge number of findings in ATC - that are all marked as Error. This is logical, if you want to identify the criticality as "what do I need to change to get into ABAP Cloud", but at the same time the important technical debt might be hidden behind a lot of "false positive" findings.

To improve this situation, SAP released the new 3 Tier check (SAP Note 3565942) in February of 2025. This check improves the situation by analyzing "classic" coding with a stronger focus on technical debt, to identify the "right" things. It is based on SAP's released objects (Tier 1, official APIs) and nominated Classic APIs. These Classic APIs belong either to business logic (e.g. BAPI_PO_CREATE1) or a technical framework (e.g. ALV). Finally, there are now also "noAPIs" (objects that must not be used, e.g. RFC_READ_TABLE) nominated by SAP.

Still - we saw a gap in projects to improve the lifes of lead developers: A custom classification. Because SAP may not be able to classify a certain ABAP object as precise as you may be able to do. So SAP is not publishing any information on a given ABAP object and it is shown as accessing an internal object (which leads in ATC to a warning). But in your use case, you are fine with using it. And this is where Kernseife can help you.

 

What is "Kernseife"?

Kernseife consists of two parts:

  1. A custom ATC Check that is based on the new Tier 3 check mentioned above, but uses a custom classification system.
  2. CAP Application to create a custom classification of ABAP objects (and we even use it to analyze results from systems in a simple way).

ATC Check for custom classification

As mentioned, we started to work on "Kernseife" in 2024 as there was no detailed Tier 3 check available then. Therefore, our focus was initially to create a custom ATC check that can work with a custom classification. It is now re-worked and based on the "new" ATC Check (Note 3565942).

This check is basically analyzing your custom code like the Tier 3 check, but when it comes to the evaluation of the impact of a finding, the custom classification comes into play. You can upload the custom classification as a JSON file and in this classification will then used to determine your findings. Let's explain the difference with an example:

In your project, you need to work with the RF framework, because you are implementing EWM in your Private Cloud Environment. Therefore, you need to work with the classes /SCWM/CL_RF*, that are the recommended way to interact with the RF framework. If you look into the Cloudifcation Repostory, you can see that these classes are not released or nominated as "classic API". Still, you want to generally accept the usage of the RF framework for both: Governance of developers (ATC Checks) and measuring your "distance to clean core".

By using your custom classification which contains the classes /SCWM/CL_RF* as "Business Function - Recommended" (BF1), which will lead to only "Information" (Prio 3) findings in the check and will have a lower impact on the "clean core score", you can improve your efficiency.

For details on the custom classification, you can check the classification guide.

Application to manage Classifications

Of course, you could maintain the JSON file of the classification manually based on the given json format. But we created a little app, that is currently in a feature branch, to do that in a more sophisticated way.

In this application, you can initially check the current state for objects as defined by SAP and additionally add objects that are not yet mentioned by SAP.

Sticking with the example from above, you can now edit the "Rating Code" of the /SCWM/CL_RF* classes to "BF1". After you did your changes, you can download the classification JSON file, upload it into your ATC check system and all of the findings for these classes will in future be recorded as "Information" (Prio 3) message.

 

Governance and Measurability

With this custom classification and the usage in ABAP Test Cockpit, we can now combine the governance view and the measurability within ATC.

Governance

By enhancing SAP's default rating, you can control your developers more efficiently. Because as of today, your lead developers that are able to approve exemptions might be overwhelmed by a lot of "false positive" ATC findings. Just imagine every usage of the example classes "/SCWM/CL_RF*" need to be exempted.

In projects - especially looking at the overwhelming errors from the "ABAP_CLOUD_READINESS" ATC check in classic ABAP, you can now control with your custom classification, that recurring "false positive" findings will disappear (or become prio 3 messages only).

This is important, as - being a reviewer - your chances to ask questions on the important findings will improve. Because if for every major developments you need to approve hundreds of exemption requests, you will most probably go into "autopilot" approval mode as you cannot check all of the requests in detail.

Measurability

Our second motivation to create this check is to create a more realistic impact rating to measure what we call the "Kernseife Score" (basically: a technical debt score per object). With every classification (BF1), we consider a score of how severe the impact of using this SAP object is with regards to clean core.

So you can configure now a score - we did use a 0 to 10 scale - to identify how severe is a single finding. Based on the ATC check, we can now provide per object a score that you can use for your reporting. Either to identify the total score per system, per package, per object - or even to deep dive into analyses like "which object has the worst average score per finding?" or "which object is in total view the "uncleanest" object in my system?".

While you can then easily use your analytics tools (e.g. SAP Analytics Cloud) to create the dashboards you need, in our repository you can at least find a CDS-View you can use for extraction (ZKNSF_I_SCORING), that combines the findings with the score. If you want to directly extract e.g. a list aggregated by object, you could directly use another CDS-View (ZKNSF_I_DEVELOPMENT_OBJECTS).

 

What's next?

Kernseife is currently in use (or in prior versions) in several projects. But to be very clear: There is development ongoing, but we do not have a product roadmap available.

But as it is open source, we are open to feature discussions or pull requests - or just use it for your own advantage!

entry.title: SAP Document AI — The Roadmap for Seamless Document Processing in SAP Business Suite
entry.content:
In today's digital world, efficient document processing has become an essential aspect of business operations. SAP Document AI is at the forefront of this transformation, providing innovative solutions that enhance productivity and streamline processes. Read more about the recent announcement here.

Upcoming Excellence with SAP Document AI Premium Plus 

Looking ahead, the SAP Document AI promises to simplify business document processing throughout SAP Business Suite through new, diverse and value-adding integration scenarios. For this purpose we plan to introduce the Premium Plus edition later this year. This edition harnesses the power of the latest generative AI technology, provides multi language support, offers turn-key integrations for enriched processing capabilities, instant learning capabilities, and harmonized experience across SAP’s portfolio.

 As part of the upcoming 2508 release of SAP Cloud ERP Public, the Premium Plus capabilities will enhance document efficiency with following embedded scenarios:

  • Quality Management: Automate the processing of incoming Supplier Quality Certificates to ensure quality compliance quickly and accurately.
  • Order Management: Expedite sales operations with automatic extraction of sales orders from documents in a variety of languages.
  • Inventory Management: Streamline inventory management by extracting key data from delivery notes for seamless tracking.

Users will benefit from end-to-end document processing automation, beginning with document ingestion, through AI processing, and culminating in matching with corresponding business objects within SAP Cloud ERP. All scenarios are also planned to be ported to the private edition soon [1].

For a hands-on look, watch the demo video depicting the processing of incoming supplier quality certificates in Quality Management:

Outlook and Roadmap for the entire SAP Business Suite

SAP’s vision for document processing integrates seamlessly into the current landscape and extends into the future. Future integrations of SAP Document AI include:

  • SAP SuccessFactors: Pre-configured content for high-volume HR documents like "National ID validation during New Hire Onboarding."
  • SAP Fieldglass: Efficiently upload and process Statements of Work within Services Procurement, simplifying document extraction and drafting.
  • SAP Enterprise Service Management: Specialized Document AI capabilities for business documents posting, including payment advice, into S/4HANA.
  • SAP Build Process Automation: Access to Generative AI capabilities for template-free document automation.
  • SAP Ariba Central Invoice Management: Enhanced invoice data extraction capabilities for superior invoice capture.

SAP Document AI continues to redefine document processing, offering businesses enhanced capabilities that drive efficiency, reduce errors, and empower organizations to focus on strategic tasks. Stay tuned and join our community of users on SAP Community. Stay updated on upcoming AI use case releases by exploring the SAP Roadmap Explorer. Explore more about SAP Document AI by visiting http://www.sap.com/ai/document-ai and don’t miss out on SAP's latest AI innovations at Sapphire 2025.

 

Author's note:

1. SAP S/4HANA Cloud Private Edition users can already leverage SAP Document AI capabilities featuring Generative AI, notably by subscribing to and integrating with SAP Document AI Premium Edition via SAP BTP.

2. SAP Document AI, previously known as Document Information Extraction, has been available in variety of integrative scenarios across SAP as a "base" capability e.g. SAP Concur Invoice Management, SAP Cash Application, SAP Build Process Automation, SAP Ariba Central Invoice Management and many more – check out this blog post for more details.

entry.title: Creating the Shell System for SAP Business Transformation Center: A Practical Approach
entry.content:
Lean Selective Data Transition (Lean SDT) with SAP Business Transformation Center (BTC) supports the transition of data that is initially analyzed and reduced to the amount that is relevant for continuing business in the new SAP S/4HANA system.

To use this transition path, a so-called shell copy of the source SAP ECC system is required — containing only customizing without application data, such as master or transactional data. This shell system is then converted to at least SAP S/4HANA 2023 or SAP S/4HANA Private Cloud Edition.

In this blog, I will provide initial insights into the shell creation process and illustrate it with a practical example.

General Approach

A copy of the source system is required to create the shell (1). This can be achieved using virtualization, third-party tools, or — as in my case — a classical system copy.

Once the system copy is complete, the client designated for SAP Business Transformation Center is deleted, recreated, and copied using a client copy with a customizing-only profile (2).

Let’s take a closer look at this approach.

Initial Situation

In this example, I used an SAP ERP 6.0 EhP8 system with the System ID (SID) SRC as the source system. It contained transactional data in client 300 and was running on an AnyDB database.

To simplify the process and reduce the number of steps involved, I opted for a heterogeneous system copy, migrating directly to an SAP HANA database. The migration key required for my customer number had already been generated in advance.

For the client copy, I performed a remote client copy. Depending on the scenario, a local client copy or client export/import could also be valid alternatives.

DISCLAIMER: 
This blog shows selected steps tailored to my specific setup. When creating your own shell system, you should always follow the official SAP documentation for System Copy and Client Copy procedures, and prepare your environment accordingly. Depending on the size of the system or the connected systems, it may be advisable to consider an alternative option.

Step 1: Generate DDL Statements (Source)

To prepare for the migration of non-standard database objects, I first logged into the source system (client 000), opened transaction SE38, and executed the report SMIGR_CREATE_DDL.

In the dialog, I selected SAP HANA Database as the target and specified the installation directory before running the report.

The DDL statements have been successfully generated and stored in the specified directory.



Step 2: Export the Database Instance (Source)

First, I copied, extracted, and launched the Software Provisioning Manager (SWPM) on the source system.

On the Welcome screen, I navigated to: SAP Business Suite 7i 2016 > EHP8 for SAP ERP 6.0 ABAP > {AnyDB} > System Copy > Source System > Based on AS ABAP and then selected the Database Instance Export option.

I followed the wizard, providing the necessary inputs, such as the Export Location.

As the Target Database Type, I selected SAP HANA Database.

Next, I supplied the SQL files generated in Step 1.

Using mostly default settings, I reviewed the summary and started the export process.

After some time, the procedure completed successfully without any issues.

Finally, I copied the database export to the shell system.

Step 3: Install the Target System (Shell)

First, I transferred the Software Provisioning Manager and required installation media (SAP Kernel, SAP IGS and helper applications, SAP Host Agent, SAP HANA database and client, ...) to the shell system. I then extracted and launched the Software Provisioning Manager.

On the Welcome screen, I navigated to: SAP Business Suite 7i 2016 > EHP8 for SAP ERP 6.0 ABAP > SAP HANA Database > System Copy > Target System > Standard System > Based on AS ABAP and then selected the Standard System installation option.

I followed the wizard, retaining the same System ID (SID) as for the source system.

ADDITIONAL INFO: 
It is also possible to use a new System ID. In this specific example, however, I chose to retain the original System ID.

Next, I used the Standard System Copy / Migration (Load-Based) option.

Since the SAP HANA database was not pre-installed on the host, I configured the Software Provisioning Manager to install it as part of the standard system installation. To enable the installation, I specified localhost as the Database Host and changed the Database ID (DBSID) to HDB.

Next, I specified the Migration Export (MIGEXPORT) copied earlier from the source system.

Using mostly default settings, I reviewed the summary and started the system installation.

After some time, the procedure completed successfully without any issues. I completed the required post-processing tasks, including disabling the SAP Cloud ALM push provider, as it is not required for this system.



Step 4: Delete the Client (Shell)

Before running the client copy with the customizing profile, it is necessary to delete the existing client 300 on the shell system.

To do this, I temporarily enabled the SAP* user (login/no_automatic_user_sapstar = false) on the shell system, logged into client 300 as SAP*, and opened transaction SCC5.

The client was already pre-selected. I enabled the Delete entry from T000 checkbox and scheduled the job to run immediately in the background.

In transaction SCC3, I monitored the deletion process until it was completed.



Step 5: Recreate the Client (Shell)

As a next step, it is required to create the client 300 again in the shell system.

I logged into client 000, opened transaction SCC4, switched to Edit mode, and created the new client 300 using New Entries.

After saving, the new client was successfully created.



Step 6: Perform Remote Client Copy (Shell)

With the new client in place, I logged into client 300 as the SAP* user. I then created an RFC destination (type 3) to the source system, using the Fast serializer as a special option (CLIENTCOPY_SRCCLNT300).

In transaction SCC9, I configured a remote client copy using the profile SAP_UCSV (Customizing, User Master Records and User Variants), selected the defined RFC destination, and scheduled the job to run immediately in the background. The customizing data began to transfer to the shell system.

ADDITIONAL INFO: 
It is possible to use other customizing profiles instead of SAP_UCSV. In this example, I explicitly chose to include user master records and user variants.

Using transaction SCC3, I monitored the remote copy process until it was completed.

The shell system has been successfully created.

Summary & Next Steps

I hope, with this blog, I was able to share initial insights on the shell copy with you and provide you with a practical guide to create the system for SAP Business Transformation Center. When creating the shell copy yourself, it is essential to carefully plan your scenario and select the option that best fits your specific system landscape and project requirements.

With the shell system successfully created, it can now be converted using the standard path to SAP S/4HANA 2023 or SAP S/4HANA Private Cloud Edition. In my case, I proceeded first with Custom Code Migration, created the Deletion Transport Request, and then converted the system to SAP S/4HANA 2023 FPS03.

entry.title: Clean Core in SAP S/4HANA: Best Practices for Future-Proof Extensions
entry.content:
The “Clean Core” approach aims to minimize custom code within the digital core of SAP S/4HANA. Instead of modifying standard SAP objects, organizations are encouraged to extend functionality through well-defined APIs, events, and standardized extension points—either within the system (in-app) or outside of it (side-by-side).

The benefits are clear:

  • Easier upgrades and reduced technical debt
  • Improved system performance and stability
  • Faster time-to-innovation with modern technologies
  • Stronger alignment with SAP’s cloud-first roadmap

SAP has made it clear: the cleaner your core, the more future-ready your business.

Extension Options in S/4HANA: In-App vs. Side-by-Side

SAP S/4HANA provides two primary extensibility models, each with distinct use cases.

In-App Extensions (Embedded in S/4HANA)

These are made inside the core system, using SAP-provided tools to add or adapt functionality without modifying standard code.

Common tools:

  • Key User Extensibility (Custom Fields, UI adaptations)
  • ABAP RESTful Application Programming Model (RAP)
  • Business Add-Ins (BAdIs)
  • Fiori App Extensions

Best for:

  • Simple UI customizations
  • Adding fields to standard reports
  • Enhancing workflows or business rules
  • Light logic tightly coupled to standard objects

Benefits:

  • Seamless user experience
  • Lower latency
  • Tight integration with S/4HANA processes

Limitations:

  • Still resides in the same system as the digital core
  • Needs regression testing with upgrades
  • Limited in scope for complex or non-SAP integrations

Side-by-Side Extensions (On SAP BTP or External Platforms)

These are built outside the core system, often on the SAP Business Technology Platform (BTP), and communicate with S/4HANA via APIs and events.

Common tools:

  • SAP BTP & Cloud Foundry
  • Cloud Application Programming Model (CAP)
  • SAP Event Mesh & API Management
  • Node.js, Java, or other open languages

Best for:

  • Complex business applications
  • AI/ML services and integrations
  • Multi-system data orchestration
  • Customer portals and mobile apps

Benefits:

  • Fully decoupled from the digital core
  • Minimal impact on upgrades
  • Uses modern cloud-native technologies
  • Scales independently

Limitations:

  • More complex to architect and monitor
  • Requires integration governance
  • Latency may be a factor for real-time scenarios

Best Practices for Clean Core Extensibility

To keep your S/4HANA system lean, modern, and agile, follow these best practices:

  1. Default to Side-by-Side for Custom Applications
    Unless you need tight coupling to a UI or standard process, side-by-side is the safer, more scalable choice.
  2. Use SAP-Provided APIs and CDS Views
    Build on standard services whenever possible to avoid low-level custom code.
  3. Adopt RAP for In-App Enhancements
    If in-app logic is needed, use the ABAP RESTful Application Programming Model to align with SAP’s future direction.
  4. Avoid Classic Modifications and Z-Code in the Core
    Stay away from code that alters SAP-delivered objects. If you must, document and isolate it with ATC (ABAP Test Cockpit).
  5. Apply DevOps and CI/CD for All Extensions
    Treat both in-app and side-by-side extensions with modern lifecycle management, including Git-based version control and automated testing.
  6. Govern APIs and Events Carefully
    Set up a clear governance model for exposing and consuming services to ensure security, versioning, and traceability.
  7. Think Long-Term and Modular
    Design loosely coupled services that can evolve independently. Avoid single monolithic apps that recreate the very bloat Clean Core is meant to avoid.

Real-World Example

A Florida based moving company, wanted to customize its product master data screens with additional attributes for regional compliance and to help customers compare FL moving rates. Instead of modifying standard SAP transactions, the team used Key User tools to add fields (in-app), and built a custom compliance rules engine as a microservice on BTP (side-by-side), triggered via events.

The result? A system that was extensible, upgrade-safe, and ready for future integration with external regulators and AI-driven recommendations.

Clean Core Is Not Optional—It’s Inevitable

As SAP shifts more innovation to the cloud and encourages customers to adopt RISE with SAP, Clean Core isn’t just a best practice—it’s a strategic imperative. Keeping your core system clean ensures you can take advantage of SAP’s latest capabilities without being stuck in a cycle of code refactoring and broken upgrades.

Clean Core enables composability, resilience, and speed—the very traits that define successful digital enterprises.

So the next time you scope out an enhancement to your SAP landscape, ask: Does this keep our core clean? If not, it might be time to rethink the architecture.

entry.title: Note-Oriented Discovery of Maintenance Planner for SAP Security Notes
entry.content:
With the May release, SAP has rolled out the new feature Note-Oriented Discovery in the Maintenance Planner.

This feature offers an option within the search interface for analyzing the SAP Security Notes that need to be implemented in the systems in your responsibility. It covers ABAP, Java, and HANA systems.

For example, a list of all productive SIDs and the Security Notes with a CVSS score > 9.5 that still need to be implemented can be generated:

Furthermore, is is also possible to search for all SIDs where a specific SAP Security Note is still pending implementation by entering the note number in the search mask. This can be helpful to gat a quick overview in case a high severity vulnerability must be fixed and the implementation status is to be tracked. Another use case could be to gain insight in very large, distributed landscapes with multiple SolMans/FRUNs or if service providers are involved.

Preconditions

One precondition is that all systems send telemetry data to the SAP Support Backbone.

To identify whether all system's data is recent, you can navigate in the Maintenance Planner to Explore Systems. The systems sending telemetry data are listed with their 'Last Synchronized On' (last uploaded to LMDB from SLD) and ' Last Replicated on' (last updated in Maintenance Planner - I assume only updated if there have been changes) date or even with an 'Outdated' indicator below the SID:

If the data is not current, please verify the process of data sending depending on your scenario, either via SolMan, FRUN, or directly.

Spoiler
For the ones interested in the details of sending data to the SAP Support Backbone, I recommend reading https://community.sap.com/t5/technology-blog-posts-by-sap/system-data-upload-to-the-sap-support-portal-via-landscape-information/ba-p/13452237. 

Furthermore, in the data collection, it must be ensured the SAP Note implementation status (Note number and implemented version) is also collected and sent to SAP. For this

  1. Go to transaction RZ70.
  2. Click 'Show/Hide Details'.
  3. Make sure '_SLD_INSTCORR' is enabled.
  4. For '_SLD_INSTCORR', set the flag in column Display and click 'Start SLD Data Collection Now' and verify if a list of notes is show. If not, go back to RZ70 and click 'Proposal' next to the list of Data Collection Programs' and repeat the test.
  5.  For '_SLD_INSTCORR', remove the flag in column Display.
  6. It should look like this:

Big thanks to @huberda for verifying the whole use case and coming up lightning fast with the solution for missing SAP Note data!

 

This new feature brings us one step closer to get features replaced before the EoL of the SAP Solution Manager.

entry.title: Simplify the downtime-optimized System Conversion to SAP S/4HANA with Customizing on Temp Instance
entry.content:
---

TL;DR 

Software Update Manager’s downtime-optimized conversion approach significantly reduces downtime during a system conversion to SAP S/4HANA. This approach is now significantly improved, resulting in fewer conversion cycles which means reducing the overall project runtime.

The key idea here is making the required Customizing settings on a temporary instance. This approach allows you to adjust the required Customizing settings during a downtime-optimized conversion run, therefore eliminating the need for additional standard conversion cycles. 

---

 

If you want to convert your system to SAP S/4HANA (also sometime referred to as brownfield approach), you're talking Software Update Manager. This tool is well-known to SAP Basis and Technology folks as it is the standard tooling for SAP system maintenance.

Software Update Manager also covers the important conversion step from SAP ERP to SAP S/4HANA. If you’re familiar with system conversions, or are already in the planning phase, you know by now that it is NOT just another upgrade. It is not only a technical step to enable the system to consume latest technologies (AI is calling!), but also requires thorough preparation at application level. And in addition, using Software Update Manger for the conversion might result in a longer downtime than your business is willing to accept. 

 

Recap of downtime-optimized Conversion

But here's the amazing news: There is a downtime-optimized approach available with Software Update Manager that moves activities that normally require the business to be stopped, into an uptime window in which the Software Update Manager works in parallel to the ongoing business.

More information on this approach called downtime-optimized conversion is available here.

The magic of this approach, in addition to utilizing the capabilities of downtime-optimized Database Migration Option (DMO) - lies in moving data conversion activities to uptime. This even includes activities which normally have to be executed manually after the conversion with Software Update Manager is complete. This reduces downtime significantly. The image below illustrates the standard conversion, the block FIN (Finance) & ML (Material Ledger) Data Conversion represents the manual activities.

 

 With a downtime-optimized conversion, the process looks like this:

 

The big downside...

HOWEVER, there is one major challenge: To automate the manual FIN&ML conversion activities, Software Update Manager requires information on what to do. To provide this information, a standard conversion run is required that also includes the manual FIN&ML conversion. This is typically done on a copy of the production system. The resulting Customizing settings are saved in a customer transport, which is then included in the next downtime-optimized Software Update Manager run.

Doesn't sound like a challenge? Well - that's true. But the Knackpunkt (excuse my German, crucial point simply doesn't sound dramatic enough 🤔) arises with time. As the initial standard conversion that runs on a copy of production and the actual production go-live are often some months apart, the Customizing on the production system very likely changes in the course of time.

And the result ⁉️

The content of the initial Customizing transport and the actual existing Customizing in the production system no longer match. If you were to run a downtime-optimized conversion using said Customizing transport Software Update Manager would apply the "old" Customizing, overwriting any changes on production that are not reflected in the customer transport.

Obviously, one solution is to copy production again to a sandbox system, complete a standard conversion run and afterwards manually execute the FIN&ML conversion activities which result in a new, updated Customizing transport.

☹️This basically means, that any downtime-optimized conversion cycle (e.g. sandbox, load verification, dress rehearsal, go-live) needs a preceding standard conversion run - which massively bloats the project plan. This is a major drawback of the approach as it leads to a longer project runtime and therefore introduces complexity to the planning.

Well, I should say this has been a major drawback...

 

... mitigated with Customizing on Temporary Instance

As the downtime-optimized conversion approach is constantly evolving it also covers a solution for the outdated Customizing issue. With Customizing on Temporary Instance, Software Update Manager offers the possibility to adapt the respective Customizing during the downtime-optimized conversion run itself - eliminating the need for an additional standard cycle. Note that the first standard conversion cycle is still required.

The following screenshot of Software Update Manager shows the dialog where you confirm that the relevant Customizing activities are complete (version 2.0 SP23 PL0):

In this phase, your application experts can log on to the temporary instance (which "reuses" of the shadow instance). The temporary instance exists on the same host with the same SID as the original instance but has a different instance number (the shadow instance number chosen in an earlier Software Update Manager dialog).

Once the Customizing settings have been adjusted, the downtime-optimized conversion run continues. These Customizing settings are saved in a transport which can then be added to the list of customer transports that are provided to the Software Update Manager in the upcoming downtime-optimized conversion cycles - ensuring that the latest changes are reflected. 

 

Additional Resources

To get started with your transition to SAP S/4HANA with this optimized flavor of the system conversion, here's a collection of information:

  • Support Portal Page of Software Update Manager: https://support.sap.com/en/tools/software-logistics-tools/software-update-manager.html
  • Support Portal Page of downtime-optimized conversion approach of Software Update Manager: https://support.sap.com/en/tools/software-logistics-tools/software-update-manager/downtime-optimized-conversion-approach.html
  • Guide for downtime-optimized Conversion with Software Update Manager: https://help.sap.com/docs/help/c4ebc2b5d928446180d9ad2667f11faa/c603616d71104e36a48330be959ed8dc.html?locale=en-US 
  • Additional information on Customizing on Temporary Instance: SAP Note 3444013 - Executing and Monitoring Downtime-Optimized Conversion to SAP S/4HANA with Customizing On Temporary Instance

Let us know what you think and feel free to comment below!

For any technical issues, please open an SAP case on component BC-UPG-DTM-TLA.

All the best for your project!

entry.title: How to debug an error message during S/4 HANA Billing document creation(VF01)
entry.content:
Hi everyone,

Have you ever been stuck during the billing document creation with an error message, and you couldn't debug it?

Let's imagine that you are using transaction VF01, you have entered a SD document, and the system only issues the error message "No billing documents were generated. Please see the log".

This is not enough for you, so you hit the "Log", and system will display the actual error raised.
In this scenario, we have error with Message ID "VF", Message class "017".
If you simply try to add a breakpoint to Message ID "VF", Message class "017" on debugger, system won't stop at program that issued this message.
This is caused by the fact that the Billing errors are stored into table VBFS, instead of using statement MESSAGE, in order to not stop the Billing document creation via batch job, but still shows the user the error to why that SD document was not invoiced.

To debug this message, you can add a breakpoint into include LV60AA98. This include is related to form VBFS_HINZUFUEGEN, called by Billing programs to issue the error on the programs of package VF.
Then, after adding the breakpoint and starting the billing creation again, you can go one step back on the call stack to find the program/logic that have called this include.

In this example, the system have issued VF017 because it failed on the validation of the "Billing status"(VBUK-FKSTK), so it called VBFS_HINZUFUEGEN to store the error.
This error is related to a Copy control routine, but this error is also issued for regular program messages like VF188/VF056(related to billing cancellation error).

If you are adding some non-standard logic to billing document creation, make sure to always store the errors in VBFS via VBFS_HINZUFUEGEN(LV60AA98).
You may also check SAP KBA 2107348 - Whole billing job cancelled due to a message in a single sales document that explains what happens if you issue a MESSAGE during a batch job.

I hope this quick explanation can ease your analysis of the billing errors during debugging!

entry.title: Taking Flight with SAP: Marco's Journey to SAP Champion
entry.content:
“In a world that is constantly reinventing itself, I enjoy collecting and passing on knowledge and always thinking one step ahead.”  That mindset has guided Marco Büscher from his early days supporting flight operations in the military to becoming a highly skilled SAP consultant and solution architect.  

Over the past 15 years, his drive to understand how various business processes fit together has led him to master multiple SAP solutions. What started as a curiosity has evolved into expertise, and Marco now thrives in the dynamic world of business informatics, constantly learning and growing, both as a professional and a mentor. 

Marco’s SAP journey began after his time in the German Armed Forces, where he gained invaluable insights into flight operations. It was there he realized the importance of understanding complex systems from all angles, a lesson he later applied to his work with SAP software. He first encountered SAP solutions in his professional life as a business IT specialist, quickly recognizing how the system’s many modules could integrate to manage business processes effectively. 

A Personal Learning Sabbatical 

Over the years, Marco’s curiosity pushed him to continually expand his knowledge. “I received my first certification in 2009, when SAP S/4HANA was still a long way off,” he recalls. “Over the years, SAP has reinvented itself again and again”. To keep up with the evolving technology, he decided to take what he calls a “learning sabbatical”. He used this time to focus on deepening his expertise in SAP solutions and acquiring multiple SAP Certifications in ABAP and SAP S/4HANA solutions.  

“My certifications form the basis for my work as a consultant and technical architect at NTT DATA Deutschland SE,” he explains. “I utilize this experience to lead the way as a pioneer, provide guidance as a navigator and offer promising solutions as a designer in a complex and ever-changing landscape”.  

Marco credits SAP Learning Hub for providing clear, well-structured, guided learning resources that helped him to get and stay certified: “The learning journey was the best way for me to learn SAP,” he adds. From his perspective, upskilling and training is the only way to unlock the full potential of SAP software. 

"By acquiring knowledge in all key SAP solutions and combining it with my understanding of the system from a developer’s perspective, I am now able to satisfy customer requirements in both standard and customized SAP solutions”. 

A Leader and Mentor in the SAP Community 

Marco’s journey didn’t stop at mastering the technical aspects of SAP. Today, he is not only an expert in his field but also a mentor, sharing his knowledge and experiences with others who are looking to build their careers in SAP.  Through his active involvement in the SAP Community, particularly his participation in SAP CodeJams, he has become recognized as an SAP Champion, a role that fills him with pride.  

As he continues to guide others, he’s not just solving complex problems—he’s helping them see the bigger picture and empowering them to chart their own success in SAP. 

The Power of Continuous Learning 

Marco’s story is a testament to the power of continuous learning and self-improvement. His curiosity, commitment, and the willingness to learn new things has led him to embrace the full potential of SAP and seek out opportunities for growth. In doing so, Marco has not only shaped his own career but is also making a lasting impact on others, as is the goal of every SAP Champion. 

Accelerate your Career Today 

You too can benefit from tailored and self-paced learning opportunities that meet you when and where you learn best on SAP Learning site. Get certified to demonstrate your expertise and boost your career.

entry.title: Parallel universes: the dual nature of UX and AI among SAP customers at Sapphire 2025
entry.content:
Sapphire 2025 was interesting and revealing. In the keynotes, theater sessions, and ASUG breakouts, SAP experts and executives talked about the latest innovations around artificial intelligence (AI), data, and user experience (UX). The customer questions and survey responses, however, revealed a parallel universe. Many organizations are on such old systems that they are not able to consume SAP’s cool new functionality. In this post, I will discuss some of my observations, based on conversations with customers and partners, and share some polling I did in one of my sessions on the intersection between SAP Fiori and AI. I will also include links to several slide decks, recorded demos, and on-demand sessions where you can dive deeper into UX and AI topics.

Customers are starting to use AI, but still behind on SAP Fiori UX adoption

In ERP1612, “Enhancing User Experience with SAP Fiori and AI”, I asked the audience of around 120 people how they are using AI in their companies. 96 people responded.

 

The 56% percent of the people who are using AI in some way is a big jump from the 37% who responded at ASUG Tech Connect last fall. The main topic in both sessions was SAP Fiori, so the audience profile should be somewhat similar, although the Tech Connect attendees might be more technical as opposed to the business focus at Sapphire.

View the slides from ERP1612.

https://www.slideshare.net/slideshow/embed_code/key/1T0gHqRT6X9ddr?hostedIn=slideshare&page=upload

The central theme was SAP Fiori, so it made sense to level set on where people are on their adoption journey. My expectations were high since SAP Fiori has been available since 2013. Since then, our team has spent many hours talking to 1000s of customers and partners about building SAP Fiori apps and how to streamline the process using our tools and technologies such as SAP Fiori elements, SAP Fiori tools, and new AI accelerators.

But, the reality was very different. Only 9 of 109 respondents are using SAP Fiori across their organizations. 43% are still in the planning stage.

 

 

To help customers move to SAP Business Suite, we had many sessions. One that’s available on demand is “Embark on a successful transformation journey to SAP Business Suite,” featuring Maura Hameroff, Priyadarsana Panda, and Soeren Ruder.

Arpan Shah describes how SAP Business Suite drives transformational value through the suite’s strength and software-as-a-service qualities with integrated processes, data, and AI in the on-demand session “Leverage the benefits of SAP Business Suite in your cloud strategy.”

There were some nice demos of the latest SAP user experience in “SAP's user experience strategy to achieve better outcomes” by Arin Bhowmik and Emil Voutta.

Tom Reiss also had some great sessions on SAP Fiori innovations with nice demos, but unfortunately, they were not recorded.

Dr. Tom Reiss before his session.

AI in SAP Cloud ERP wowed the crowd

In Chris Hoffmann’s session, BAI1362, which I heard several people say was the best talk of the entire Sapphire, he showed several key innovations in SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition. Here are the slides and demos from his presentation:

  • Slides in PDF format
  • Insights card creation with Joule
  • AI-assisted easy filter
  • AI-assisted smart summarization
  • AI-assisted error explanation
  • AI-assisted smart solution for situations in My Home
  • SAP Fiori elements Project Accelerator

Chris Hoffmann during his talk about adding AI to SAP Fiori apps at both design time and runtime

Several executives from SAP and our customers discussed how AI is transforming business. See their session “Harness the benefits of data and AI in ERP and drive innovation” on demand.

For a bigger picture view on where these AI innovations fit into the broader SAP Business Suite, watch “Transform your enterprise with the power of SAP Business Suite.”

SAP Sapphire is a great place for discovery

The new (to SAP) harmonized product operating model focuses on discovery. Interacting with customers to understand their needs plays a big role for product managers and everyone involved in the development process. Sapphire and the after-hours events provide a great venue for conversations with customers about the problems they want to solve.

Since I’m known for my interest in user experience topics, most of my meetings and questions in my sessions focused on UX and adjacent topics. One of my goals was to gather feedback on what we show and address questions about what people need. While people seemed to like the new AI functionality in SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition that Chris and I showed in our respective sessions, the most common question was when it is coming to private cloud (planned) and on-prem (not planned).

There were several sessions on organizational change management, which seemed to be one of the main reasons that people have not migrated off the SAP GUI, even though they like to complain about it. Some of the main takeaways from the OCM panel were:

  • Prioritize people not technology
  • Change management takes a toll on people – prep for the after.
  • Don’t lose sight of the goal and decommission the old technology. Make sure to listen and be agile.
  • The journey will be difficult, acknowledge this and over-communicate.

I really enjoyed Paul Saunders’ entertaining and informative sessions on “Leveraging dark data for enhanced decision-making” and “Antifragile enterprises: How true resilience comes from embracing change.” These too were not recorded.

The panel on antifragile enterprises: How true resilience comes from embracing change

Too much content; too little time

Content overload was a common theme I heard from many of the people I spoke with at Sapphire. There were so many sessions crammed into the compressed 2-day format (compared to three days in years past).

BTP remained a hot topic, with many people looking to understand the best way to obtain value.

Discover SAP Business Technology Platform and its value for your business is a good overview on this.

Another good BTP session is “Unleash the power of your applications, data, and AI with SAP BTP.”

AI everywhere – separating reality from hype

AI was top of mind for everyone as it played such a major role in the keynotes. The most common issues that people raised with me were:

  • Concerns about privacy of their data
  • How much of the capability is real and can the results be trusted?
  • Availability of AI functionality outside of SAP Cloud ERP and the associated costs

SAP Sapphire is all about the people

It was great to meet several colleagues in person for the first time and to connect with those that I see only a few times each year.

If we did not meet at Sapphire and you are interested in joining the conversation about SAP Fiori application development and how AI makes it better, faster, and cheaper, please join our monthly SAP Fiori development roundtable group or sign up for our bi-monthly newsletter.

For the UX Foundation team, Peter Spielvogel.

entry.title: EWM & QM integration - Quality inspection after GR from production
entry.content:
INTRODUCTION
This guide will demonstrate how to set up and perform a quality inspection after goods receipt from production using EWM. The process involves the advanced production integration with EWM and the Quality Inspection Engine (QIE) on the EWM side.

PROCESS OVERVIEW
Let's consider a scenario in a hypothetical warehouse. The goods are produced, and the production team creates the handling unit using an RF device. The quality inspection must take place before the final putaway.

ASSUMPTIONS
We assume that the advanced production integration between EWM and PP is already in place. 

Step 1: Receiving HU with RFUI
The handling units are received in EWM. The user scans the production order and confirms the HU creation using standard menu 3464. After that, an inbound delivery is automatically created and should be visible at /SCWM/PRDI.

Step 2: Create WT to QM Work Center
Next, create a warehouse task (WT) to move the HU from the GR-PROD storage bin to the quality inspection work center.

Step 3: Process Usage Decision
Perform the usage decision using transaction code /SCWM/QINSP_S4. If the results are positive, a WT is created to move the HU to the final storage according to the follow-up actions.

Step 4: Confirm Final Putaway
Finally, confirm the WT to move the HU from the work center to the final storage bin.

CUSTOMIZING STEPS

QM steps:
- Generate Inspection Object Type Versions
- Maintain Inspection Object Types Version
- Maintain Inspection Rule Group
- Define and Activate Warehouse-Dependent IOTs
- Define Follow-Up Actions
- Assign Logistical Follow-Up Actions
- Assign Default Work Center for Inspections 

EWM steps:
- Define Process-Oriented Storage Control
- Define Warehouse Process Type

Master data steps:
- Maintain material master - MM01)
- Define Master Data Attributes - /SCWM/TWORKST
- Define inspection rule - /SCWM/QRSETUP

All the steps above will be detailed in this blog. The following steps are necessary to set up the quality inspection process in EWM. First, generate the IOT objects:

  • SAP Customizing Implementation Guide -> SCM Extended Warehouse Management -> Extended Warehouse Management -> Cross-Process Settings -> Quality Management -> Basics and Integration -> Generate Inspection Object Type Versions


  • SAP Customizing Implementation Guide -> SCM Extended Warehouse Management -> Extended Warehouse Management -> Cross-Process Settings -> Quality Management -> Basics and Integration -> Maintain Inspection Object Types Version


  • SAP Customizing Implementation Guide -> SCM Extended Warehouse Management -> Extended Warehouse Management -> Cross-Process Settings -> Quality Management -> Inspection Planning -> Maintain Inspection Rule Group

  • SAP Customizing Implementation Guide -> SCM Extended Warehouse Management -> Extended Warehouse Management -> Cross-Process Settings -> Quality Management -> Basics and Integration -> Define and Activate Warehouse-Dependent IOTs


  • SAP Customizing Implementation Guide -> SCM Extended Warehouse Management -> Extended Warehouse Management -> Cross-Process Settings -> Quality Management -> Inspection Results -> Maintain Follow-Up Actions - > Define Follow-Up Actions

    If you want to customize the follow-up actions, you can assign your function module with custom logic to meet your specific business requirements, especially if the standard processes are not sufficient.

  • SAP Customizing Implementation Guide -> SCM Extended Warehouse Management -> Extended Warehouse Management -> Cross-Process Settings -> Quality Management -> Inspection Results -> Maintain Follow-Up Actions - > Assign Logistical Follow-Up Actions

    The customizing below will set the stock type to unrestricted “FF” after the usage decision (UD) is completed using transaction code /SCWM/QINSP_S4.

  • SAP Customizing Implementation Guide -> SCM Extended Warehouse Management -> Extended Warehouse Management -> Cross-Process Settings -> Quality Management -> Inspection Results -> Assign Default Work Center for Inspections


    After the goods receipt, a process-oriented storage control (POSC) will be executed to handle the quality inspection and final putaway. To enable this, the following steps must be in place:

  • SAP Customizing Implementation Guide -> SCM Extended Warehouse Management -> Extended Warehouse Management -> Cross-Process Settings -> Warehouse Task -> Define Process-Oriented Storage Control

    Start defining the external steps that will be used and assign them to an internal process step as follows:

    Then create a new Storage Process, for our example we created the “ZIPQ”:

    Then, assign the external steps to the storage process as follows: the first step handles the quality inspection at the work center, and the second step manages the warehouse task for the final putaway.

    The storage process (POSC) should be assigned to the WPT used for the goods receipt process, following the customizing below

  • SAP Customizing Implementation Guide -> SCM Extended Warehouse Management -> Extended Warehouse Management -> Cross-Process Settings -> Warehouse Task -> Define Warehouse Process Type



MASTER DATA:
After completing the customizing steps some master data must be maintained so the process works. The first step is to maintain and activate the inspection type at the material master (MM02).

  • T-Code: MM02 - Maintain material master data

    Another master data that should be in place is the work center attributes. Here you must maintain the storage bin as follows:

  • T-Code: /SCWM/TWORKST
    Menu: Extended Warehouse Management -> Master Data -> Work Center -> Define Master Data Attributes
    Make sure the storage bin for the Quality Work Center is filled. If it's not, you won't be able to do any follow-up actions after making a usage decision. If you don't, you'll see the following error:

    Message no. /SCWM/QUI_WRKST099 HU 112345678000003606 has no external step for the storage process control assigned

    The last master data step will be creating an inspection rule on the EWM side to trigger the inspection lot creation.

  • T-Code: /SCWM/QRSETUP
    Menu: Extended Warehouse Management -> Master Data -> Quality Management -> Maintain Inspection Rule


TESTING

  • Step 1 – Receiving HU with RFUI
    T-Code: /SCWM/RFUI

    The inbound delivery was created automatically after the HU creation using RFUI:

  • Step 2 – Create WT to QM Work Center
    T-Code: /SCWM/PRDI

    This step can be automated using a PPF action to create and confirm the WT, but in this case, we will perform it manually:

    During the HU-WT creation, we can see that the storage process “ZIPQ” was set according to our customizing:
    NOTE: Confirm the WT using RFUI or manually using foreground confirmation in /SCWM/MON for test purposes.

    After creating the WT, the inspection lot will be automatically created as we can see in the EWM monitor.

  • Step 3 – Step 3: Process Usage Decision
    T-Code: /SCWM/QINSP_S4

    Open the quality inspection work center, select the inspection lot created, and make a usage decision.

    After UD, goods receipt is performed as we can see in the production order:

    Also, the WT to the final putaway is created based on the follow-up actions:
    NOTE: Confirm the WT using RFUI or manually using foreground confirmation in /SCWM/MON for test purposes.

CONCLUSION
Using the Quality Inspection Engine (QIE) within EWM is highly recommended because it is integrated with warehouse processes, providing real-time data and automation that streamlines inspections directly within the warehouse environment. This integration reduces manual effort, minimizes errors, and ensures immediate updates to stock status and quality results

I try to make it simple, hope this guide helps you guys.

Best Regards,

Yuri Nogueira
SAP EWM Consultant

entry.title: SAP Enterprise Support Academy Newsletter June 2025
entry.content:
Dear customers,

The SAP Enterprise Support Academy provides expert guidance and high impact knowledge transfer services that enable you to innovate and be successful with your SAP solutions.

entry.title: What’s New in SAP Build: Highlights from SAP Sapphire 2025
entry.content:
As the SAP Sapphire 2025 global keynote outlined, this year's focus was clear: AI, applications, and data. SAP Build continues to evolve in alignment with these themes, helping organizations efficiently build, automate, and extend their business processes and applications.

Here's a roundup of Sapphire's key SAP Build announcements and innovations to watch this year.

Joule Studio: Intelligent AI Agents and Skills Builder

Joule Studio Capabilities

SAP unveiled Joule Studio, a new environment within SAP Build that helps you create, deploy, and manage AI agents and custom skills. With Joule Studio, teams can enrich Joule with company-specific skills and intelligent agents that work across SAP and third-party systems. Joule Studio brings two powerful capabilities:

  • Joule Skills: Design modular, reusable skills that can be invoked during conversations. These skills can trigger actions, pull data, and orchestrate SAP Build components like workflows, automations, or apps.
  • AI Agents: Go beyond skills by building full-fledged AI agents that dynamically execute business tasks using reasoning capabilities. These agents use business context, data from SAP systems, and large language models to adapt to user needs.

Joule Studio will be available by the end of Q2 2025, with Agent Builder capabilities expected in Q4 2025.

Early adopters like Sovanta, an SAP partner, already use Joule Studio to enhance customer service. Sovanta significantly streamlined support processes and accelerated ticket resolution by integrating conversational skills with SAP systems.

Another early adopter, Pricefx - a global leader in cloud-based pricing software - is using Joule Studio to bring AI-driven intelligence into their solution. By developing an Agentic AI copilot, they aim to deliver contextual, business-aware guidance that enhances pricing decisions for their customers.

Interested in learning more? Register for the webcast: Meet Joule Studio: Drive Innovation with AI Agents & Joule Skills.

New AI Capabilities for Business Users and Developers

  • SAP Build Work Zone + Joule: Employees can now use Joule to find relevant apps, tasks, and documents faster. It can summarize or generate content based on your business data, saving time and boosting productivity.

Joule in SAP Build Work Zone

  • AI-Enhanced ABAP Development: Developers migrating to SAP Cloud ERP can use AI to convert custom ECC code into ABAP Cloud standards. This feature simplifies modernization efforts while ensuring code quality and compliance.

These capabilities are expected in Q3 2025.

 Expanded SAP Build Capabilities

SAP Build continues to grow as a unified platform with:

  • Extensibility Wizard for SAP Business Suite: A guided wizard that helps you build side-by-side extensions with the proper context, faster. SAP S/4HANA Cloud Private Edition and SAP SuccessFactors support is planned for release in H2 of 2025.

Extensibility Wizard in SAP Business Suite

Watch Sapphire Customer Session: Extending SAP S/4HANA Cloud with SAP Build at OGE: Open Grid Europe used SAP Build to extend SAP S/4HANA Cloud—boosting onboarding efficiency by 50% and reducing invoicing time by 20%.

Watch Sapphire Solution Demo: Extend SAP S/4HANA Cloud: SAP Build application development and automation. Discover how to rapidly personalize and extend SAP S/4HANA Cloud while maintaining a clean core.

  • SAP Business Data Cloud Integration: Planned for Q4 2025, SAP Build will offer deep integration with SAP Business Data Cloud (BDC) via CAP. Developers can consume and expose data products using virtual tables in SAP HANA Cloud, accelerating the development of insight-rich apps while reducing manual integration work. This improves data consistency and supports scalable, compliant development.
  • Event-Driven Applications: SAP Build, in combination with SAP Integration Suite and CAP, will support event-based interactions across systems. General availability is expected in H2 2025.

Simplified Pricing and Packaging

  • SAP Build offers a unified pricing model that combines app development, automation, and workspaces under a single entitlement. This simplifies adoption, reduces licensing complexity, and allows teams to build and extend at their own pace. Learn more in this blog post.

Thanks for reading! Which SAP Build innovation are you most excited about? Let us know - and don't miss these key resources from SAP Sapphire 2025:

  • Watch Sapphire Global Keynote: Your Best. Made Real. No Matter What Lies Ahead
  • Read SAP Sapphire 2025 Innovation Guide
  • Visit the Session Catalog with On-Demand Sessions

entry.title: Do you want to learn all about Production Planning (PP) and Detailed Scheduling (DS) optimizers?
entry.content:
I recommend to access our Planning with Advanced Planning Methods in ePPSDS learning journey. 

Overview
This course is designed to provide detailed insights into advanced planning methods within SAP S/4HANA PP/DS. The course focuses on the Production Planning (PP) and Detailed Scheduling (DS) optimizers, their application and configuration. In addition the course touches on heuristics.

Learning objectives

  • Provide detailed insights into the application and configuration of the PP and DS optimizers.

Prerequisites

Essential:

  • Basic business knowledge of processes in manufacturing (production planning and execution)

Recommended:

  • S4200 (Business Processes in SAP S/4HANA Manufacturing)
  • S4224 (Production Planning with SAP S/4HANA PP/DS)

Please post you question related to the digital learning Journey in the Q&A area. 
Our SAP Learning Experts will get back to you as soon as possible! We are here to support you. 

We appreciate your feedback to make sure we continue sharing interesting topics.

Kind regards
Susann

entry.title: 2025 ASUG Best Practices for Oil, Gas, and Energy
entry.content:
Snapshot of dynamic leaders engaged in keynote/panel discussion at last year's ASUG Best Practices 

Goodness is coming! 

Our 2025 ASUG Best Practices for Oil, Gas, and Energy is set for October 6-8th, 2025.  For 2025, the event returns to its singular focus on the Oil, Gas, & Energy industry, no longer co-located and combined with other industries as was the offering of this event in 2024. Additionally for 2025, we return to the hospitable city of Houston, Texas, where the Hilton Americas-Houston will serve as the venue for the 2025 ASUG Best Practices for Oil, Gas, and Energy event.

Proceed now with informing and inviting your customers to join us in Houston for this premier gathering of customers, partners, and professionals within the SAP ecosystem.  The event will enable all to connect with peers, learn from industry experts, and discover how to drive transformative results in their organization.  The event kicks-off with an empowering and enlightening keynote from ExxonMobil’s, Kurt Aerts, Director, Plastics and Product Life Cycle.   The keynote from Kurt Aerts will not only inform and inspire, but it will set the stage for what we anticipate will be an event and overall conference experienced saturated with goodness.  In addition to the opening keynote, Day 1 will provide a welcoming reception that is sure to give all the opportunity to connect with industry leaders and fellow attendees. Day 2 continues with the goodness, featuring keynotes, customer journeys, deep dive sessions, and an insider's look into SAP roadmaps.   Day 3, being the final day of the event will cap off the experience with expert sessions, dynamic discussions, and hands-on learning opportunities.  Throughout the event exciting customer stories, partner sessions, and talks by experts will be shared, equipping participants with the knowledge and tools to:

  • Optimize Asset Management and Lifecycle Strategies: Discover SAP solutions to maximize the value of your investments and extend asset lifecycles, ensuring operational efficiency across various industries.
  • Enhance Operational Agility and Resilience: Learn how to implement best practices for streamlined operations, enabling your organization to adapt quickly to market fluctuations and disruptions.
  •  Strengthen Data Security and Governance: Explore SAP's capabilities for robust data security practices, compliance, and effective data governance strategies.
  • Optimize Supply Chain Efficiency: Gain insights into digital supply chain management strategies that enhance efficiency and resilience and are adaptable to a wide range of industries.
  • Empower Your Workforce with Advanced SAP Technologies: Master the latest SAP tools and techniques to improve user experience, drive workforce productivity, and foster innovation.

The customer line-up that will grace the stage at the event telling their amazing stories of transformation and SAP's role in enabling and facilitating that transformation include, just to list a few, the following:  ExxonMobil, BPx Energy, Chevron, Precision Drilling, Merit Energy, CITGO Petroleum, Southwest Gas, Oxy, Messer.

Indeed, goodness is coming, so get ready and plan to be present at what will be an enriching and enjoyable event of pure SAP Oil, Gas, & Energy goodness!  Access the LIVE Agenda!  Register Now!

We look forward to seeing and connecting with you in Houston, Texas, October 6 - 8, 2025 for the 2025 ASUG Best Practices for Oil, Gas, and Energy conference that is certainly poised to position organizations to be their best.

entry.title: Connecting S/4HANA On-Premise to SAP Advanced Event Mesh: A Matter of Trust!
entry.content:
Hello SAP Community,

When connecting S/4HANA On-Premise to SAP Advanced Event Mesh, many of the configuration steps are well-documented. This blog post is intended to complement the primary setup blog. We will take a deep dive into what is often the most challenging part of the process: establishing mutual trust between AEM and S/4HANA through proper certificate management.

This connection is a crucial step towards building a robust, event-driven architecture. It allows your core business processes to publish and consume events, enabling real-time responsiveness and decoupled integrations. This guide provides a straightforward, step-by-step approach to get your S/4HANA system and AEM communicating, with a special focus on the certificate handling.

Prerequisites

Before we begin, ensure you have the following:

  • Access to your S/4HANA On-Premise system with authorization for transactions STRUST, SMICM, and /IWXBE/CONFIG.
  • An active SAP Advanced Event Mesh instance running in your SAP BTP subaccount.
  • The AEM Validation service must be active in the same BTP subaccount where your AEM instance resides. This is key for the setup.

Part 1: Preparing S/4HANA Certificates in STRUST

The first step is to establish a proper identity for your S/4HANA system by replacing the default dummy certificates. All of this is done in transaction STRUST.

  1. Navigate to STRUST: Open the transaction STRUST. You will see several PSE (Personal Security Environment) nodes on the left. We will primarily focus on "SSL Server Standard" and "SSL Client SSL Client (Standard)".

     
     

  2. Load CA Root Certificates: For each PSE, you need to load the necessary CA root and intermediate certificates. You can obtain the necessary SAP Root CA certificates from your corporate certificate authority. Add them to the certificate least one by one.

  3. Create a Certificate Signing Request (CSR):

    • Right-click on a node (e.g. in our case, "SSL Server Standard") and choose "Replace". 
    • Key in the details of your server e.g. CN=mys4.mydomain.com and so on.
    • Save the PSE for your client.
    • Click the "Create Certificate Request" button.
    • Copy the generated CSR text.

  4. Sign the CSR: Submit this CSR to your corporate Certificate Authority to receive a signed certificate response.

  5. Import the Certificate Response:

    • Back in STRUST, with the corresponding node selected, click the "Import Certificate Response" button.
    • Paste the response you received from the CA.
    • You should see the chain of certificates similar to that image below.

  6. Repeat for the SSL Client: Repeat steps 3-5 for the "SSL Client SSL Client (Standard)" PSE. This client certificate is crucial as it will be used to authenticate S/4HANA against Advanced Event Mesh. Note: The screenshots just show the client, it's the same for the server.

  7. Save: It's good practice to save the certificates to the database certificate store for persistence.

Part 2: Establishing Two-Way Trust

For a successful connection, S/4HANA must trust AEM, and AEM must trust S/4HANA. This is achieved by exchanging certificates.

Step 2.1: AEM Trusts S/4HANA

We need to provide AEM with the public key of the S/4HANA client certificate we configured in Part 1.

  1. Export S/4HANA Client Certificates:

    • In STRUST, navigate to the "SSL Client SSL Client (Standard)" node.
    • You should see your certificate chain (Your Client Cert -> Intermediate CA -> Root CA).
    • Export each of these three certificates one by one in Base64 (PEM) format.

  2. Import into AEM:

    • Log in to your AEM Cluster Manager.
    • Navigate to your Broker, click Manage, and go to the Authentication and Security section.
    • Select the Certificate Authorities tab.
    • Upload the three certificate files you exported from S/4HANA. You should have three new entries when you are done.

Step 2.2: S/4HANA Trusts AEM

Now, we must configure S/4HANA to trust the certificates presented by the AEM broker endpoint.

  1. Get the AEM Broker Certificates:

    • The easiest way to do this is by using your web browser. Navigate to your AEM Broker Manager URL (https://*.messaging.solace.cloud).
    • Click the padlock icon in the browser's address bar and view the certificate chain.
    • Export the entire chain. Typically, this includes a root CA (e.g., "DigiCert Global Root G2"), an intermediate CA (e.g., "Thawte TLS RSA CA G1"), and the server certificate itself (e.g., *.messaging.solace.cloud).
    • Note: Your specific CA names may differ.
    • You can see int the screenshot below that by navigating up and down you can export all 3 certificates (here we just need the lower 2).

  2. Import into S/4HANA:

    • In STRUST, navigate to the "SSL Client SSL Client (Standard)" node.
    • Import the AEM root and intermediate CA certificates you just downloaded into the certificate list. This ensures any certificate signed by this CA will be trusted.

Part 3: Configuring the Connection

With trust established, we can now configure the connection itself.

  1. Enable Client Certificate Auth in AEM:

    • Inside your AEM Broker Manager, navigate to Messaging -> Access Control -> Client Authentication.
    • Enable the "Client Certificate Authentication" option.

     

  2. Create a Client Username in AEM:

    • Still in the Broker Manager, create a new Client Username.
    • Crucially, the name must exactly match the Common Name (CN) of your S/4HANA "SSL Client SSL Client (Standard)" certificate from STRUST.

     

  3. Create the Channel in S/4HANA:

    • Go to transaction /IWXBE/CONFIG.
    • Click the "Create new channel via Service Key" button.
    • In the drop-down menu, select "SAP Advanced Event Mesh".

  4. Use the Service Key:

    • In your BTP subaccount, find the service instance for the AEM Validation service.
    • Create a service key and copy the JSON content.
    • Paste this service key into the dialog in S/4HANA.

    The system will use the service key to automatically configure the necessary RFC destination and connection details. Perform a connection test to validate!

Troubleshooting: The Dreaded SSL Error

The most common issue is the SSL certificate not trusted (ICM_HTTP_SSL_PEER_CERT_UNTRUSTED) error during a connection test. Here’s how to pinpoint the problem:

  1. Activate ICM Trace:

    • Go to transaction SMICM.
    • From the menu, choose Goto -> Trace -> Reset -> All Files. This gives you a clean slate.
    • Set the trace level to 2 or 3 and check the box for "Trace encrypted data".

  2. Reproduce the Error: Go back to /IWXBE/CONFIG and run the connection test again to trigger the error.

  3. Analyze the Trace:

    • In SMICM, go to Goto -> Trace -> Display File.
    • Search for *** ERROR. You will find a section detailing the validation failure.

    Here is a real-world example from a trace file:

    BEGIN VERIFICATION RESULT
#   Messages
ERROR: The chain of certificates is incomplete or untrusted, missing certificate of [C8:02:5F:9F] CN=DigiCert Global G2 TLS RSA SHA2
#   Summary
#01 Certificate (End Entity): VALID
 Subject:           CN=*.cf.jp10.hana.ondemand.com, O=SAP SE...
 Issuer:            CN=DigiCert Global G2 TLS RSA SHA256 2020 CA1, O=DigiCert Inc, C=US
 PKI validation:    FAILED: Validation of dependents - Issuer Certificate (Issuer - Only Invalid Certificates Found)
...
cli SSL session PSE "/usr/sap/S4H/D00/sec/SAPSSLC.pse"
    • Interpretation: The ERROR: line tells you exactly which certificate is missing (CN=DigiCert Global G2...). The line
      cli SSL session PSE, confirms that the system is using the Client PSE (SAPSSLC.pse), so you know you need to upload the missing certificate into the "SSL Client SSL Client (Standard)" node in STRUST.

Conclusion

By carefully setting up the certificates to establish mutual trust and then using the service key for automatic configuration, you can create a stable and secure connection between S/4HANA On-Premise and SAP Advanced Event Mesh. This unlocks powerful capabilities for your enterprise.

I hope this detailed walkthrough helps you in your integration journey. Feel free to leave questions or share your own experiences in the comments below!

entry.title: Improve BP master data with SAP MDG DQM to reduce superfluous costs in invoicing processes
entry.content:
Introduction:

In invoicing processes with suppliers and customers, bank account information such as account number and IBAN play an important role. If this information is not correctly maintained in the business partner master data, costs arise from faulty bank transactions, and business partner relationships may also be strained due to delayed payment processes.

With the help of SAP MDG DQM validations and derivations, such situations can be avoided. A fully automatic reconciliation between account numbers and IBAN can be carried out. Initially, erroneous data records are detected through a DQM validation rule and a DQM evaluation run. Subsequently, these data records can be automatically corrected via an MDG mass process.

Background:

Depending on the configuration and history of the SAP S/4HANA system, account information and IBAN numbers are stored in different database tables. Account numbers and other details are found in the BUT0BK table. Associated IBAN numbers are stored in the TIBAN table. More detailed information on this is well described in the SAP Community Blog SAP S/4HANA Business Partner: Handling of IBAN with CVI.

When new business partners are created using SAP S/4HANA and/or SAP MDG, the information is correctly written into the tables, and no action is required. However, due to the migration of legacy data, interfaces, and data transfers, it can happen that the IBAN numbers, which contain the account number, do not match the dedicated account numbers in the BUT0BK table. In payment transactions, incorrect or improperly formatted account numbers are then used, and bank transactions can fail.

With the help of the SAP MDG DQM validation rule explained here, such erroneous BUT0BK entries are detected. The DQM rule will use a standard function module via BRF+ to calculate the correct bank account numbers from the IBAN numbers. A DQM evaluation run will iterate over all BUT0BK entries and identify erroneous records according to the rule. The user can then send these erroneous records to a mass process. This will automatically correct the account number in BUT0BK through a derivation step. Here, too, the SAP standard function module for calculation is reused.

DQM Validation Rule

The validation rule is created using the SAP MDG app "Validations Rules for Business Partners." The base table is the table BUT0BK, and the field being checked is BUT0BK-BANKN.

The following screenshot shows the rule in the app:

The scope of the rule defines which records are checked. It is important that only records with an IBAN in the bank details are checked. Therefore, the scope expression includes a BRF+ artifact to read the IBAN from TIBAN using the function module READ_IBAN_EXT through the parameters of the BUT0BK table. If an entry/IBAN is found, the record can be validated and is "in scope."

The following screenshot shows this scope expression:

The condition expression itself compares the value from BUT0BK-BANKN with the result of the function module CONVERT_IBAN_2_BANK_ACCOUNT. If the values are different, it means that there is an error for this data record. The following screenshot shows the condition expression.

 

 

 

DQM Derivation Scenario / Rule

 

The derivation rule is very similar to the validation rule and uses the same function blocks. Instead of detecting an error, a derivation can also write or correct the value.

In this case, an extra derivation scenario was defined for this demo in the SAP MDG DQM app "Define Derivation Scenario for Business Partners." The name is, for example, "Correct Bank Account Numbers." The base table is BUT0BK, as in this case the BANKN field of this table is corrected. The scenario only requires one rule at the field level. Additional rules are not necessary.

As with the validation rule, the scenario scope can also act on the presence of an IBAN for the bank account number. This ensures that only relevant records are processed in the derivation scenario.

The rule on the BANKN field is relatively simple to define: There are no condition fields, and only the BANKN field is defined as the result field. The screenshot shows the rule in the app "Define Derivation Scenario for Business Partners."

 

The decision table CALC_BANKN_FLD_DT generated by the SAP MDG framework also uses the SAP standard function modules to calculate the correct value for the bank account number via the IBAN. The following screenshot shows the details:

 

All DQM artifacts must be approved and activated after implementation. Additionally, the rule itself must be set to the status "Execution – YES".

Running the scenarios

 Execute Evaluation Run

If no automatic execution of the evaluation run is scheduled, it can be started via the SAP MDG app "Evaluate Data Quality."

 

 

Review of the evaluation run

With the app "Evaluation Results for Business Partners," the result can be examined in detail. By applying a filter to the rule, the erroneous data sets can be isolated and viewed. In the following screenshot, you can see that 7 business partners do not comply with the rule:

 

Remediation process with Mass Processing

To initiate a remediation process for one or more datasets, use the "Process" functionality directly from the "Evaluation Results for Business Partners" app.

In the following screenshot, 2 datasets are placed into a mass process.

 

In the following pop-up screen, the process template to be executed can be selected.

Info: This popup is only available in Cloud Ready Mode. In classic mode, the standard template is always used.

During the process, the derivation scenario is executed. The following screenshot shows the process after the derivation rule. It can be seen that the two incorrect bank accounts in BUT0BK have been corrected.

It is also apparent that a business partner has obviously defined multiple bank accounts, and one of them was already correct. Therefore, it was not overwritten.

Rerun the evaluation process.

If the evaluation run is triggered again, the data quality will have improved because the two corrected data sets will no longer be recognized as errors.

Compare the result with point 1: only 5 bank accounts are still incorrect.

Hope this MDG scenarios makes sense for you and please give feedback. In case of questions don’t hesitate to add them to this blog

Best Regards

Steffen

SAP Master Data Governance SAP Data Quality Management

entry.title: How Signavio Process Analysis and Mining helps to deal with shortage of SAP ERP consultants
entry.content:
Many people argue about the shortage of S/4 consultants and limited knowledge and how to run and optimize SAP processes in an ever changing IT world.
